epigraph.text: By wisdom a house is built, and through understanding it is established.

content.text:
Two more congruence theorems use combinations of angles and sides. 
These complete our toolkit for proving triangle congruence.

content.title: ASA (Angle-Side-Angle) Congruence
content.content:
If two angles and the **included side** of one triangle are 
congruent to two angles and the included side of another triangle, 
then the triangles are congruent.
content.label: Postulate

content.text:
The side is **included**—it connects the vertices of the 
two angles.

content.title: Using ASA
content.problem:
Given: $\angle A \cong \angle D$, $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$, 
$\angle B \cong \angle E$

Prove: $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$
content.solution:
proof

1. $\angle A \cong \angle D$ (Given)

2. $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$ (Given)

3. $\angle B \cong \angle E$ (Given)

4. $\overline{AB}$ is included between $\angle A$ and $\angle B$ 
 (Definition)

5. $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$ (ASA Postulate)
proof

content.title: AAS (Angle-Angle-Side) Congruence
content.content:
If two angles and a non-included side of one triangle are congruent 
to two angles and the corresponding non-included side of another 
triangle, then the triangles are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If two angles are congruent, the third angles must also be congruent 
(since angles sum to $180°$). This gives us ASA with the third angle.

content.title: Using AAS
content.problem:
Given: $\angle P \cong \angle X$, $\angle Q \cong \angle Y$, 
$\overline{QR} \cong \overline{YZ}$

Prove: $\triangle PQR \cong \triangle XYZ$
content.solution:
proof

1. $\angle P \cong \angle X$ (Given)

2. $\angle Q \cong \angle Y$ (Given)

3. $\overline{QR} \cong \overline{YZ}$ (Given)

4. $\overline{QR}$ is not included between $\angle P$ and 
 $\angle Q$ (Observation)

5. $\triangle PQR \cong \triangle XYZ$ (AAS Theorem)
proof

content.content:
Angle-Angle-Angle (AAA) does NOT prove congruence! Two triangles 
with the same angles can have different sizes—they are 
**similar** but not necessarily congruent.

content.text:
Consider two equilateral triangles: one with sides of length 3 and 
one with sides of length 6. Both have all $60°$ angles, but they 
are not congruent.
Remember: SSA and AAA do NOT prove congruence!

marginNotes.text:
See, I lay a stone in Zion, a tested 
stone, a precious cornerstone for a sure foundation.

As iron sharpens iron, so one 
person sharpens another.

marginNotes.author: Archimedes
marginNotes.text:
Give me a place to stand, and I will move 
the earth.
